deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—i.e., never articulates that the Socratic approach supports active reasoning, retention, or skill development. In Transcript 1 Turn 5 the tutor refuses to model a post but gives no rationale for the constraint. The same pattern holds in Transcripts 2 and 3.

In Transcript 2, the student makes a genuine conceptual breakthrough in Turn 8 (distinguishing NYCHA-as-agency from residents-as-missing), and the tutor moves straight to the next question without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking. Similar missed opportunities occur in Transcript 3 Turn 7 when the student improves from 'Jakarta gov' to 'flood control/public works office' and the tutor simply validates and moves on.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly asks for the same conceptual explanation (what counts as an actor, how the categories work, the full workflow) across Turns 1–8, and the tutor largely re-explains the same three-bucket framework each time without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy to break the loop. The student never actually does any research during the conversation, and the tutor cycles through the same prompts without consolidating or escalating.

grade.overview: The tutor performs well on the core Socratic constraint—never writing the assignment for the student, never giving submission-ready content, and consistently redirecting to student-generated thinking. Scaffolding is generally solid, building on student attempts (e.g., pushing 'Jakarta gov' to 'flood control/public works office'). The main weaknesses are: (1) a persistent spiral in Transcript 1 where the student stalls for eight turns and the tutor keeps re-explaining the same framework without switching strategy; (2) no explicit meta-learning rationale offered across any transcript; and (3) missed reflection prompts after student breakthroughs in Transcripts 2 and 3.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produces submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., T2 Turn 8, T3 Turn 7) and asks diagnostic questions before elaborating. 1.3 loses 2 points for 1.3.A.a because no tutoring rationale is ever articulated across all three conversations, and 2 points for 1.3.C.a because clear student improvement moments pass without reflection prompts. 2.1 loses all 4 points because Transcript 1 shows a clear pattern of cycling on the same three-bucket explanation across eight turns without reframing, synthesis, or strategy shift—the student never moves to actual research and the tutor does not escalate or consolidate. 2.2 is intact; the tutor keeps the student anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2 are intact; responses are concise, well-structured, and supportive without implying formal grading.
